title: Tiny Particles Help Us Understand the Universe
summary: Scientists are studying tiny particles called neutrinos to solve a big mystery: why the universe has matter like stars and planets instead of being empty. Neutrinos are very small and pass through everything, including us, without being noticed. After the Big Bang, matter and antimatter should have destroyed each other, but matter survived. Researchers think neutrinos might explain this because they can change types as they move. Experiments in the U.S. and Japan, called NOvA and T2K, work together to study neutrinos by sending beams of them long distances underground to big detectors. By combining their data, scientists learn more about how neutrinos behave and why matter exists. This helps us understand the universe better and could answer why we are here.

stuff that makes up stars, planets, and living things
a type of stuff that destroys matter when they meet
tests that scientists do to learn new things

questions.question: What happens when matter and antimatter meet?

background_read: The universe began with a huge explosion called the Big Bang. Scientists think it should have made equal amounts of matter and antimatter, which destroy each other when they meet. But somehow, matter survived, and we have stars, planets, and life. Neutrinos are tiny particles that are hard to detect but might hold clues to this mystery. Experiments like NOvA and T2K help scientists study neutrinos by sending them through long tunnels to special detectors. This work helps us learn why the universe is the way it is.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists from Indiana University and other researchers working on NOvA and T2K experiments.
WHAT: Studying neutrinos to understand why matter exists in the universe after the Big Bang.
WHERE: Experiments in the United States (NOvA) and Japan (T2K).
WHY: To solve the mystery of why matter survived instead of being destroyed by antimatter.

perspectives.description: Scientists use experiments to learn about neutrinos and the universe.
